result:
I want to write about silence. Not the absence of sound, but the fullness of it. The kind that settles in a room after a deep conversation, or in a forest at the precise moment the wind holds its breath. We live in a world that treats silence as a vacuum, an empty space to be filled at all costs with podcasts, playlists, notifications, and the low-grade hum of our own anxious thoughts. We fear it, I think. We confuse it with loneliness, with boredom, with irrelevance. But what if silence isn’t an endpoint, but a doorway?

I remember the first time I experienced true, unadulterated silence. I was maybe ten years old, staying at my grandfather’s old farmhouse in the middle of a snowstorm. The power went out, as it often did. The furnace stopped its rhythmic gurgling. The fridge fell mute. The television, a blank grey eye. And then, the outside world vanished under a foot of fresh, heavy snow, swallowing every distant car sound, every barking dog. I sat on the living room floor, wrapped in a quilt, and I heard it. It wasn’t nothing. It was a presence. It had weight and texture. It was the sound of my own heartbeat in my ears, the faint, papery rustle of my own breathing. In that silence, the small flame of the oil lamp on the table didn’t just provide light; it performed a silent, dancing opera. The shadows it cast on the wall were profound and alive. That silence didn’t empty the room; it filled it to the brim with a different kind of substance. I was not bored. I was, for perhaps the first time, completely present.

We have so few places to find that now. Even our attempts at “quiet” are curated. Noise-cancelling headphones don’t lead us to silence; they lead us to a private auditory chamber of our own choosing. We swap the chaos of the world for the chaos of our playlists. There’s a value in that, of course—the power to sculpt our sonic environment is a modern marvel. But it’s not the same. It’s a managed experience. True silence is unmanaged. It is wild. It doesn’t ask you what you want to hear; it asks you to listen to what is already there, beneath the surface of everything.

And what is there? In the external silence, the internal noise amplifies. This is why we flee from it. The to-do lists, the half-formed arguments, the echoes of awkward conversations, the vague dread about the future—it all rushes into the void, clamoring for attention. The first few minutes of deliberate silence are often an exercise in tolerating this inner cacophony. It’s like stirring up the silt at the bottom of a pond. The water looks murky, chaotic. But if you can resist the urge to stick another input in, to stir it up again, something remarkable happens. The silt begins to settle. The frantic thoughts, having been acknowledged but not engaged, start to lose their urgency. They float by like clouds. And beneath them, the water clears.

In that clear space, other things emerge. Not thoughts, but perceptions. A sudden, vivid memory of the smell of rain on hot pavement when you were seven. A genuine, wordless feeling of compassion for someone you’ve been frustrated with. A creative idea that doesn’t arrive with a bang, but uncurls slowly, like a fern frond in spring. This is the silence that poets and mysters have sought for millennia. It’s the “peace that passeth all understanding.” It’s not an absence of conflict, but a foundation that exists beneath it.

I think of it as a form of listening with your whole being. When you listen to a person, truly listen, you are silent. You aren’t just waiting for your turn to speak; you are receiving them. The same is true of the world. In silence, you receive the world as it is, not as you wish to narrate it. You notice the geometry of a spiderweb beaded with morning dew. You feel the slow, ancient patience of a stone. You sense the interconnected hum of life that you are a part of, a hum that is felt, not heard. This is where awe lives. It cannot shout over the din of a TikTok scroll. It whispers. It requires the cathedral of silence to be heard.

There is also a radical, almost rebellious quality to seeking silence today. In an economy and a culture built on capturing attention, to voluntarily step away and give your attention to nothing but the raw material of existence is a quiet act of defiance. It is a declaration that your consciousness is not just a consumer, but a contemplator. That your worth is not measured in your constant engagement, but sometimes in your profound disengagement.

I am not good at this. I fill my days with sound and stimulus as much as anyone. But I have begun to build little altars to silence. The five minutes in the morning before I reach for my phone, just staring out the window at the sky. The walk without headphones, where the soundtrack is rustling leaves, distant lawnmowers, and the cadence of my own footsteps. The conscious decision, sometimes, to just drive in the car with the radio off, to be alone with the rhythm of the road.

In these small silences, I am reintroduced to myself. Not the “self” of my résumé, my social media profile, or my roles and responsibilities, but the deeper, quieter self that simply *is*. The one that existed before I had a name, and will exist, in some way, after all the noise I generate has faded. That self doesn’t speak in words. It speaks in sighs, in intuitions, in a deep sense of belonging to the fabric of things.

So, I am trying to stop seeing silence as empty space on a calendar, to be filled. I am trying to see it as the fertile ground from which everything meaningful grows. The pause between musical notes that makes the melody. the space between words that holds the meaning. The rest in a heartbeat that allows for the next pulse of life. It is not a retreat from the world, but a deeper, more resonant way of inhabiting it. In a universe that began with a unimaginable bang, perhaps our most human task is to cultivate, within our own small souls, the profound and listening silence that allows us to hear the echo of it, and everything that has followed, within ourselves.
